analytic.title: Entangled-Beam Reflectometry and Goos-Hänchen Shift

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We introduce the technique of Entangled-Beam Reflectometry for extracting spatially correlated (magnetic or non-magnetic) information from material surfaces or thin films. Our amplitude-and phase-sensitive technique exploits the coherent nature of an incoming entangled probe beam, of matter or light waves, undergoing reflection from the surface. Such reflection encodes the surface spatial structure into the probe's geometric and phase-derived Goos-Hänchen shifts, which can then be measured to unveil the structure. We investigate the way these shifts depend on the wave packet widths, and illustrate our technique in the case of in-plane periodic (non-)magnetic structures by utilizing spin-path mode-entangled neutron beams.

Introduction.-Advances in quantum materials with complex inhomogeneous structures are often thwarted by the need for tools to unravel them. Reflectometry is a non-destructive general technique to identify these structured materials that utilizes some form of light [1] or matter waves, e.g., neutrons [2][3][4], as test probes. With a broad range of modalities, such as non-polarized, polarized, specular and off-specular, methods like neutron vector magnetometry and soft resonant x-ray reflection can reveal structural features of layered magnetic materials. They are, however, limited to uncovering either statistically-averaged electromagnetic quantities or variations along particular spatial directions. Then, a natural line of inquiry is whether exploiting entanglement of the probe overcomes those limitations and, ultimately, provides additional structural information. Here, we propose to exploit the entangled Goos-Hänchen (GH) effect [5][6][7] as a novel quantum sensing strategy.

The GH-shift refers to the in-plane lateral shift, λ x , of the center of a beam of waves undergoing total reflection (TR) from a surface (along the x-axis in Fig. 1). This phenomenon (disputed to have been) suggested in light by Sir Isaac Newton [5], was measured by Goos and Hänchen in 1947 [6,7]. The first theoretical description of the phenomenon focused on the phase shift Φ(k 0z ) of an incoming plane wave of momentum k 0 = (k 0x , k 0y , k 0z ), λ ACH x ≡ k0x k0z dΦ(k0z) dk0z , an expression known as the Artmann-Carter-Hora (ACH) formula [8,9]. The connection between this and the geometric spatial shift λ x [10] was made by arguing that λ ACH x could be expressed as the effective displacement resulting from the group delay time τ the wave experiences between arrival and departure from the surface, first noted by Agudín [11], λ ACH and mv x = k 0x , assuming non-relativistic particles of mass m (and reduced Planck constant ℏ = 1). Since these original contributions various attempts at deriving such a geometric shift using wave packets have been advanced in the literature [12][13][14][15]. Recently, de Haan et al. * ortizg@iu.edu [16] claimed observation of the effect in neutron optics, but consensus remains elusive [17].

In the present work we introduce the general technique of Entangled-beam (matter or light waves) Reflectometry which leverages the GH-shift that results after the incoming beam, entangled by design in various ways, is reflected by a magnetic (or birefringent) surface slab. In conjunction with Wollaston prisms or radio-frequency flippers, the GH effect can be exploited to generate arbitrary mode-entangled neutron beams [18]. We show how entanglement of the incoming neutron beam can be used to extract information about inhomogeneous (magnetic and/or non-magnetic) structures of the probed surface. The entangled beam is a very sensitive probe of magnetism along the direction perpendicular to the surface; in particular, it is very sensitive to thin magnetic layers.

Wave Packet Width and the Goos-Hänchen Shift.-Why do the GH-shift expressions seem independent of the incoming wave packet width? After all, a geometric GH-shift has a physical meaning only for finite width wave packets, making the identification λ x = λ ACH x questionable. We next illustrate by means of an elliptical wave packet analysis that this is indeed the case. Our motivation is grounded on available experimental evidence where longitudinal, ∆ l , and transverse, ∆ t1 and ∆ t2 , coherence lengths might differ [19][20][21].
Consider an incoming wave packet in momentum space centered at r c = (x c , y c , z c ), (N is a normalization factor)
where we omit spinor notation for now, and k 0y = 0.
Here, ∆ x ∆ z = ∆ l ∆ t1 , ∆ z = ∆ 2 l sin 2 α + ∆ 2 t1 cos 2 α, and ∆ y = ∆ t2 since, without loss of generality, we have chosen the plane of incidence to be the xzplane (Fig. 1).
The Gaussian peaks at k0z =
div.formula: sin α(k 0l ∆ 2 l -kx cos α (∆ 2 l -∆ 2 t1 )) ∆ 2 z
div.p: along k z (Momenta along different frame axes are related by k x = k l cos α, k z = k l sin α). This wave packet gets reflected by a surface whose reflection coefficient R(k z ) depends only on the momentum component normal to the surface because of translation symmetry along the x and y direc-tions. For TR, the resulting reflected wave packet (we assume no absorption) can be expressed in terms of a momentum-dependent phase shift, Φ(k z ) ∈ R, Ψ (r) (k) = R(k z )Ψ (i) (k) = e iΦ(kz) Ψ (i) (k). In coordinate representation, the time(t)-dependent reflected wave function,
div.formula: Ψ (r) (r, t) = 1 (2π) dk Ψ (r) (k) e i(k (r) •r-k (r)2 2m t) ,(2)
div.p: carries information about the surface, where α < α c (critical angle), and we define k (r) = (k x , k y , -k z ).

To extract the GH-shift one needs to examine the spatial wave function. Unfortunately, the integral in Eq. (2) does not admit a closed analytic form. To proceed, we assume ∆ z to be larger than the semi-classical penetration depth δ p (k z ) = -i 2 d ln R(kz) dkz [15], and expand Φ (up to second order in k z ) about k0z
div.formula: Φ(k z ) = -i ln R + 2 δp (k z -k0z ) + W 2 (k z -k0z ) 2 , (3
div.p: where
div.formula: W 2 (k z ) = -i d 2 ln R(kz) dk 2 z
div.p: , and R, δp , W 2 are evaluated at k z = k0z . While this expansion is sufficient for the spherical wave packet case (∆ l = ∆ t1 = ∆ t2 ) [15], it is not in the elliptical case because Ψ (r) (r, t) intertwines the x and z coordinates due to the k x -dependence in k0z . Consider next Eq. ( 1) and assume that k x ≈ k 0x . Then, the quasi-spherical approximation amounts to
div.formula: ∆ 2 l -∆ 2 t1 ∆ 2 l ≪ k 0l k 0x cos α = 1 cos 2 α ,(4)
div.p: and k0z becomes
div.formula: k0z ≈ k0z = ∆ l ∆ z 2 k 0l sin α = ∆ l ∆ z 2 k 0z ,(5)
div.p: where, to lowest-order, the ellipticity in the incoming wave packet simply renormalizes the peak value k0z to k0z . This replacement, k0z → k0z , extends to all other quantities which are dependent on k0z , such as Φ, δp and W 2 . Using this quasi-spherical approximation, the integral in Eq. ( 2) now becomes separable: Ψ (r) (r, t) ≈ Ψ (i)

x (x, t)Ψ k(r) 0 = (k 0x , 0, -k0z ). We are now in a position to address our original question. From the time-evolved incoming state one can determine the moment at which the center of the wave packet reaches the surface. The time interval between the arrival of Ψ (i) 's center and the departure of Ψ (r) 's center from the surface, τ WP (the wave packet analog of the group delay time τ ), can be determined from Ψ (r) z (z, t). The geometric GH-shift λ x can then be defined as the displacement of Ψ (i)
x (x, t)'s center during the interval τ WP
div.formula: λ x = v x τ WP = 2 δp cot α ∆ z ∆ l 2 ,(6)
div.p: which shows a wave packet width dependence. Note that the second-order term, W 2 , does not affect the GH-shift. While Eq. ( 6) addresses our initial puzzle, a natural question arises whether the ACH formula still gives the same result as Eq. (6). The asymptotic reflection phaseshift, in the far-field limit (t/m ≫ ∆ 2 z , W 2 ), is Φ( k0z ) [22]. Note that this expression is different from the reflection phase shift Φ(k 0z ) of an incoming plane wave. By analogy to the plane wave case one can define the wave packet version of the ACH formula
div.formula: λ ACH x ≡ k 0x k 0z d Φ( k0z ) dk 0z = 2 δp cot α ∆ l ∆ z 2 ,(7)
div.p: which, in general, is different from the geometric shift in Eq. ( 6). For a spherical wave packet the width dependence disappears and thus Eq. ( 6) agrees with Eq. ( 7).

e ik•ξ/2 , written in the σ z -basis (|+⟩ ẑ and |-⟩ ẑ ), carries information about the displacement vector ξ separating the centers of the two incoming coherent wave packets (Fig. 1). Upon TR from a magnetic (or birefringent) sample, the reflection operator R(k z ) = diag(R + , R -), in its orthonormal eigenbasis |±⟩ n, defines an effective quantization axis n = (sin θ cos φ, sin θ sin φ, cos θ), where θ and φ are the usual Bloch sphere angles [22]. The two bases are related by a unitary unimodular rotation U(θ, φ): Hence, the reflected wave function is given by
div.formula: Ψ (r) ξ (r, t) = 1 (2π) 3 2 dk R(k z )U † Ψ (i) ξ (k) e i(k (r) •r-k (r)2 2m t)(9)
div.p: in the surface |±⟩ n-basis, and in the quasi-spherical limit can be expressed as
div.formula: Ψ (r) ξ (r, t) = µ,ν=± U * µν Ψ (r) µν (r, t) ⊗ |ν⟩ n ,(10)
div.p: where Ψ

(r) µν (r, t) has its center at r (r) µν (t) [22]. Here, indices µ and ν carry physical meanings: µ is related to the incoming spin quantization axis ê (= ẑ in Eq. ( 8)), and ν is associated to the reflected one. Hence, upon TR, an incoming µ spin state is split into Ψ Two physical effects emerge from Eq. (10). The first concerns the generated entanglement pattern. Generically, the reflected state splits coherently into four wave packets (Fig. 1), yet it remains mode-entangled with respect to the distinguishable path and spin subsystems decomposition. Secondly, if the incoming spin quantization axis ê coincides with that of the sample n, there will only be two reflected wave packets Ψ (r) ++ and Ψ (r) --, separated by a reflected entanglement vector ξ
div.formula: (r) = r ++ (t)-r --(t), with ξ (r) z = -ξ z + 2( δp+ -δp-), ξ (r) ∥ = ξ ∥ = (ξ x , ξ y ).
div.p: Hence, the distance between the two wave packet centers, i.e., the entanglement length [23], can be increased or decreased depending on the difference between penetration depths δp± . Furthermore, in this particular case one can identify a simple physical picture of the entangled GH-shift, i.e., each incoming spin state |±⟩ ẑ experiences the GH-shift λ x± shown in Fig. 1.

Measuring the Entangled Goos-Hänchen Shift. We next describe a way to extract the GH-shift from polarization measurements performed at the far-field as in Fig. 1. The (asymptotic) polarization components, P γ (ξ) = lim t→∞ P γ (ξ, t), γ = x, y, z, can be expressed as
div.formula: P γ (ξ) = µ,µ ′ ,ν,ν ′ =± U µν U * µ ′ ν ′ n⟨ν| σ γ 2 |ν ′ ⟩ n C µµ ′ νν ′ ,(11
div.p: with
div.formula: C µµ ′ νν ′ = lim t→∞ dr Ψ (r) * µν (r, t)Ψ (r) µ ′ ν ′ (r, t) = Λ µµ ′ νν ′ e iη µµ ′ νν ′
div.p: , where Λ µµ ′ νν ′ ≤ 1 encodes the overlap between reflected spin states at the detector [22], and the asymptotic reflected phase difference,
div.formula: η µµ ′ νν ′ ∈ R, is given by (∆ Φ νν ′ = Φ ν -Φ ν ′ ) η µµ ′ νν ′ = ∆ Φ νν ′ + µ -µ ′ 2 k 0 • ξ . (12
div.p: One can then use the generalized ACH formula,
div.formula: λ ACH x+ -λ ACH x- ≡ k 0x k 0z d∆ Φ νν ′ ( k0z ) dk 0z ,(13)
div.p: and from the measured phase shift ∆ Φ νν ′ ( k0z ) determine the relative GH-shift [22]. For perfectly collimated beams, had one chosen ξ along the y-axis one would have eliminated the last term in Eq. ( 12). Using a disentangler before the detector (Fig. 1) affects the ξ-dependence of the measured polarization. The ultimate goal of this resulting spin-echo entangled-beam technique is to make sure that the observed phase shift is due only to the reflection process. For instance, if one
div.formula: chooses ê = n = d, η µµ ′ νν ′ → ∆ Φ νν ′ [22].
div.p: Entangled-Beam Reflectometry.-In entangled-beam reflectometry three length scales enter into the problem, the beam diameter, the wave packet width ∆ x(y) , and the surface-projected entanglement length ξ ∥ ; we assume the beam diameter is the largest length scale. Consider first the case where the slab structural variations happen along the z-axis. There are practical advantages in using entangled beams in TR mode as opposed to traditional reflectometry techniques [3]. For instance, if the sample is non-magnetic or its effective magnetic quantization axis satisfies n = ê = d, the observed spin-echo polarization (Fig. 1) measures the spinor's reflection phase shift difference ∆ Φ+directly [22]. If that is not the case, there is a remnant ξ-dependence in the measured polarization [22]. Nonetheless, under particular experimental conditions (e.g., entangled thermal neutron beams), because of an averaging over the incoming beam divergence, one may eliminate that ξ-dependence (an example is presented in [22]). In this latter case, the main advantage of our technique over the standard polarized-beam reflectometry is in sensitivity, because the spin-echo polarization shows strong signals at larger values of the reflectivity than conventional spin asymmetry measurements. This increased sensitivity is more dramatic in the case of thin magnetic multilayers.

We next consider the novel application of our technique to probe in-plane (xy-plane in Fig. 1) inhomogeneities. Now, the characteristic period p of the surface inhomogeneities competes with the beam length scales. If the distribution of nuclear density or electromagnetic fields on the surface is inhomogeneous, i.e., V (r), then, each spinor component experiences a different potential and, consequently, a different reflection operator R(r ∥ , k z ) where r ∥ = (x, y). (Note that the sample material is three-dimensional and structural variations along z are encoded in the k z -dependence of R(r ∥ , k z ).) Does our entangled probe provide additional information? We show next that this is indeed the case.

If only the nuclear density varies (no magnetic fields), R(r ∥ , k z ) = R(r ∥ , k z ) 1, the observed spin-echo beam polarization P x (ξ ∥ , t) (ξ z = 0), averaged over incoming impact parameters (spanning the area A), is [22]
div.formula: P x (ξ ∥ , t) = 1 A R A dx c dy c dr ∥ dk z R * (r + , k z )R(r -, k z ) ×|Ψ (i) (r ∥ , k z , t)| 2 , (14
div.p: where R stands for the real part, r ± = r ∥ ± ξ ∥ /2, and
div.formula: Ψ (i) (r ∥ , k z , t) = dk ∥ 2π e i(k ∥ •r ∥ -k (r)2 2m t) Ψ (i) (k).
div.p: Eq. ( 14) can be seen as the reflection analog of a purely transmission phase diffraction grating setup, where the role of the grating transmission function is played by the modulated surface reflection coefficient R(r ∥ , k z ) [24]. This also explains why the relationship between ξ ∥ , ∆ x(y) and p is not important for this non-magnetic case. Spin-echo resolved experiments from diffraction gratings have been done in the past [25] but have been interpreted using a semi-classical theory [24] rather than Eq. ( 14).

The more challenging case presents when the in-plane inhomogeneities are magnetic. Our entangled-beam reflectometry technique is now applicable in the regime ∆ x(y) < p ≤ ξ ∥ because the incoming wave gets reflected in its locally-varied spinor basis n(r ∥ ). Here, the two spinor components effectively experience a different local reflection, R(r γ± separated by ξ ∥ . The observed beam polarization P γ (ξ) can be determined by using Eq. ( 11) with these now r (r) γ± -dependent quantities, and then average over the impact parameters [22]. Furthermore, one can vary the direction of the vector ξ in Ψ (i) ξ (k) to obtain spatial information of correlations along various directions on the surface. For perfectly collimated beams one may choose an entanglement vector ξ perpendicular to the incidence plane (ξ = ξ y ŷ in Fig. 2) to cancel the ξdependent phases in Eq. ( 12), while we expect damping in the intensity in the far field limit. In many practical applications the beam divergence is non-negligible, and one has to resort to spin-echo measurements to cancel the angular divergence of the beam as much as possible. However, in the magnetic case, spin-echo reflectometry [22] may not be straightforward since n(r ∥ ) varies on the surface, and this is not known a priori.

As an illustration consider the magnetic structures, shown in Fig. 2, which rest on semi-infinite slabs (Fig. 1). The optical potential V1 includes the nuclear potential and magnetic field B 1 (r ∥ ) = B 1 n(r ∥ ), where the magnitude B 1 is constant and the unit vector n(r ∥ ) = (θ, φ(r ∥ )), with φ(r ∥ ) periodic. To increase the contrast in P γ , the grazing angle α = 0.37 • is set close to the critical angle α c-= 0.43 • (α c+ = 0.57 • ), corresponding to the |ν⟩ n = |-⟩ n state. Alternatively, one can use specifically-engineered magnetic layers with large in-plane difference in their reflection matrices R(r ∥ , k z ). The entangled beam has ξ = ξ y ŷ. The structure in Fig. 2(a) is periodic with θ = π/2 and φ(r ∥ ) = -π/3 if y mod p a < 2 3 p a , π/3 otherwise.

. Interestingly, one can extract the period p a from P x . The asymmetry in the spatial extent of each magnetic-field region, within a period, is encoded in a non-vanishing value of P y . Inset (b) shows a two-dimensional periodic structure with θ = π 3 and φ(r ∥ ) = π sin 2πx p b cos 2πy p b . The period p b also shows up in P x , while a non-vanishing P z signifies that B 1 has an out-of-plane component.

Conclusions.-The revealed entangled Goos-Hänchen effect is a phenomenon that evinces the wave nature of light and matter, and the unique trait of quantum mechanics which Einstein epitomized as spooky. The intrinsic value of this phenomenon rests on the possibility to exploit it by designing entangled probes that, after interaction with a target surface-distribution of electromagnetic fields, unveil by interference that surface in-formation. This amplitude-and phase-sensitive technique the present work introduced is called Entangled-Beam Reflectometry and is not unique to a particular probe. The information obtained depends on the nature of the probe and the entanglement design. For instance, neutron beams mode-entangled in spin and path provide information about inhomogeneous non-magnetic or magnetic-field surface distributions, by direct encoding in spinor phase differences. While off-specular diffuse scattering has been used to study non-magnetic structures [26], due to the low intensity of the partially-reflected beam its applicability is limited, a problem we do not face in TR. By tuning the length and direction of the probe's entanglement vector, and in conjunction with spin-echo measurements, one can in principle map out the magnetism of the material's surface. Within an elliptical wave packet formulation, we proved the width dependence of the Goos-Hänchen shift, and also clarified the distinction between geometric and ACH phase shifts [8] calculations of the Goos-Hänchen shift.

The method can also be applied when the reflectivity is less than unity and, since it directly yields the relative phase between neutron spin states, it potentially allows reflectivity data to be inverted to yield the scatteringlength depth profiles [2]. Furthermore, the relative phase permits identification of thin magnetic layers that is more sensitive than traditional neutron spin asymmetry reflectometry because the measurements can be made at larger values of the reflectivity. It is our hope that this Entangled-Beam Reflectometry technique will be applied to unravel exotic electromagnetic structures, including characterization of topological materials.

div.p: FIG. 3. A ray representation of the standard GH effect, assuming α ≤ αc, the critical angle, above which the reflection is no longer total. An incoming wave of mean momentum k0 impinges on a surface at z0 and gets reflected. Geometric, λx, and phase-derived, λ ACH x , GH shifts are indicated. Allowed magnetic structures along the z-axis are simply characterized by a stratified medium with homogeneous layers (of width dj and optical potential Vj) labeled by the index j = 1, 2, • • • , N + 1. Momenta along different frame axes are related by kx = k l cos α, kz = k l sin α.
The spinor state and its first derivative in layer j are given by

div.formula: Ψ j (z j-1 ) Ψ ′ j (z j-1 ) = M j Ψ j (z j ) Ψ ′ j (z j ) ,(20)
div.p: one can connect the spinor state at coordinates z j and z j-1 . From boundary conditions at z j-1 and z j in layer j Ψ j (z j-1 ) Ψ ′ j (z j-1 ) = B j-1 (z j-1 )χ j-1 = B j (z j-1 )χ j (21)
div.formula: Ψ j (z j ) Ψ ′ j (z j ) = B j (z j )χ j = B j+1 (z j )χ j+1 ,(22)
div.p: it results
div.formula: Ψ j (z j-1 ) Ψ ′ j (z j-1 ) = B j (z j-1 )B -1 j (z j ) Ψ j (z j ) Ψ ′ j (z j ) = M j Ψ j (z j ) Ψ ′ j (z j ) ,(23)
div.p: with (d j = z jz j-1 )
div.formula: B -1 j (z) = 1 2 e -ikj z -ie -ikj z k -1 j e ikj z ie ikj z k -1 j ,(24)
div.p: and

div.head: II. ELLIPTIC WAVE PACKET DERIVATION OF GEOMETRIC AND PHASE-DERIVED GH-SHIFTS

Here, for simplicity, we assume the scalar case but the result can be straightforwardly generalized to the spinorial case. The reflected wave function is given in Eq. (9). Because this integral does not admit a closed form solution, in general, we proceed with the expansion and quasi-spherical approximation scheme of Eqs. ( 3) and (5).
Under this approximation the resulting reflected wave function, Ψ (r) (r, t) ≈ γ=x,y,z Ψ (r) γ (r γ , t), becomes separable in terms of one-dimensional Gaussians,

div.formula: φx (x, t) = - k 2 0x 2m t + k 0x (x -x c ), φy (y, t) = 0, φz (z, t) = - k2 0z 2m t -k0z (z + z c -2 δp ),(36)
div.p: where only terms to O(1/t) are kept and we have made use of k 0y = 0. Since the GH-shift is the longitudinal distance between incoming and outgoing reflection points, one can heuristically use Ψ (r) z to extract the geometric GH-shift λ x . Note that Ψ (r) z 's center is shifted by twice the penetration depth 2 δp , the factor of 2 resulting from the identification of the classical path of an incoming particle traveling into the material before leaving the surface. While this interpretation yields illuminating insights for the GH-shift, it is only a semi-classical one. Particularly, Ψ (r) z is only the solution to the Schrödinger equation for z < 0, thus it does not describe the motion of the incoming wave packet inside the material. To obtain λ x one needs to compute the time interval
div.formula: τ WP = 2 δp v z = 2 δp k0z /m ,(37)
div.p: which yields Eq. ( 6). Note that the second-order correction W 2 does not affect λ x . To measure this shift one would need a position-sensitive detector able to resolve such shift. Alternatively, one can determine the relative phaseshift of the reflected wave packet and use the generalized ACH formula. The phase shift accumulated by the , is derived from the asymptotic reflection phase-shift Φ( k0z) measured at time t → ∞.

reflected wave function Ψ (r) (r, t), Eq. ( 10), in the limit t → ∞ is the sum of all phases in Eq. ( 36). The overall accumulated phase can be easily obtained by realizing that the normalization factor in this limit becomes
div.formula: lim t→∞ N (t) → im 3 ∆ x ∆ y ∆ z π 3 2 t 2 (t -2m W 2 ) e i( Φ-2 k0z δp) ,(38)
div.p: meaning that it contributes the non-trivial phase Φ -2 k0z δp . For unentangled particles the total reflection phase shift of the incoming wave packet is given by
div.formula: lim t→∞ Arg(Ψ (r) (r, t)) - k(r) 0 • r + k(r)2 0 2m t = Φ( k0z ),(39)
div.p: where we subtracted the kinetic phase. Using this wave packet analog of the reflection phase shift with the modified ACH formula, and noting that
div.formula: d Φ( k0z ) dk 0z = d Φ( k0z ) d k0z d k0z dk 0z = 2 δp ∆ l ∆ z 2(40)
div.p: one arrives the GH-shift in Eq. ( 7) for the scalar case.

Comparison between ACH and geometric GH-shifts for different incoming states can be found in Table I. We also remark that the W 2 -correction does not affect the GH-shift. Rather, W 2 modifies the effective wave packet dispersion. For beams reflected from a semi-infinite slab with a constant optical potential W 2 = k0z δ3 p , this correction is relevant for precisionmeasurement experiments involving mirrors (as in Mach-Zehnder interferometers) that have large penetration depths δp . If the slab is magnetic, as we will see, its effect will be different on each spin component of a neutron wave packet, thus affecting the measurement contrast at the detector.

If the beam carries spin (or polarization) and the surface's optical potential couples to it, then each surface eigen spinor will generally experience different penetration depths, δp± , and thus, ultimately, different GHshifts. Here, we use the subscript ν = ± to denote the corresponding surface eigen spin state's quantities, i.e. δpν , λ xν , Φ ν and W 2 ν . Then, λ ACH x+λ ACH

x-can be observed by extracting the phase-shift difference in a reflected entangled beam from polarization measurements and using the generalized ACH formula, Eq. ( 13). (10), and where the centers are modified due to the spin-path entanglement and the spin-dependent penetration depths, i.e.,

div.p: The case of an unentangled incoming beam provides the simplest illustration. Consider a particle beam polarized along the x direction (this corresponds to putting ξ = 0 in χ (i) k•ξ ). Then,

div.p: The polarization in Eq. ( 45) assumes that the reflected waves travel freely from the surface to the detector. However, when the incoming wave packet is Ψ (i) ξ one typically positions a disentangler before detection. We show next the theory behind the effect of the disentangler on the measured polarization (Sec. IV describes the mathematics involved in spin-echo reflectometry; for the experimental realization see Ref. [29]).

Assume the particle beam is entangled with U se ê (ξ), and the disentangler, U se d (ξ (se) ), is set along the quantization axis d = n at time t = t se with entanglement vector ξ (se) = -(ξ x , ξ y , -ξ z ). We note here that this setting of ξ (se) is particular to the case of reflectometry, in contrast to the usual transmission mode, where ξ (se) = -ξ. The reason behind this is that the reflection operator has the form

Hence, in order for the disentangler to be the desired inversion of the entangler, the k z -transfer matrix part needs to be |k z ⟩ ⟨-k z |, which effectively inverts ξ z . The action of the disentangler transforms the reflected wave function as
div.formula: Ψ (r) µν (r, t se ) → Ψ (se) µν (r, t se ) = Ψ (r) µν (r + ν ξ (se) 2 , t se ). (50
div.p: Due to this shift, after leaving the disentangler Ψ (se) µν has its center and phase, from Eq. ( 41) and (42), modified to
div.formula: r (se) xµν (t) = r (r) xµ (t) -ν ξ x 2 , r (se) yµν (t) = r (r) yµ (t) -ν ξ y 2 , r (se) zµν (t) = r (r) zµν (t) + ν ξ z 2 ,(51) φ
(se) xµν (x, t) = φxµν (x, t) + ν k 0x ξ x 2 , φ(se) yµν (y, t) = 0, φ(se) zµν (z, t) = φzµν (z, t) -ν k0z ξ z 2 . (52
div.p: The polarization P γ can now be computed and the result is of the form of Eq. ( 11) with the modification Λ (se)

div.p: where ξ

div.p: We see that only the amplitude terms Λ (se) ++--and Λ (se) --++ are ξ-independent. If the exponential damping is substantial, this means that these two terms will be the main contribution to P γ . Interestingly, the phase difference η (se) ++--and η (se) --++ are also ξ-independent. Therefore, P γ might appear to not contain ξ, especially if one normalizes the P γ by purely the spin-analyzer's count, instead of measuring the reflectivity. The reflectivity because it does not have crossterms from different reflected spin-states.
Note that in those cases where ê = d = n, i.e., the perfect spin-echo situation, the measured polarization does not depend on ξ.
Different from the previous cases, when the quantization directions ê = d ̸ = n, the action of the disentagler on each Ψ (r)
div.formula: µν is now Ψ (r) µν (r, t se ) |ν⟩ → |Ψ (se) µν (t se )⟩ (54) = Ψ (r) µν (r + ξ (se) 2 , t se ) d ⟨+|ν⟩ n |+⟩ d +Ψ (r) µν (r - ξ (se) 2 , t se ) d ⟨-|ν⟩ n |-⟩ d
div.p: Now, the action of the disentangler can no longer be understood purely in terms of the wave packet center and phase shift as before. As an example, we consider explicitly the case ê = d = ẑ and n = ŷ, while the exact polarization can be calculated in closed form, the expression is cumbersome and un-enlightening, thus we give here the result in the incoming plane-wave limit
div.formula: P x = cos 2 k 0 • ξ cos ∆Φ +-+ sin 2 k 0 • ξ, P y = sin 2k 0 • ξ 1 -cos ∆Φ +- 2 ,(55)
P z = cos k 0 • ξ sin ∆Φ +-,
div.p: where P 2 x + P 2 y + P 2 z = 1 and all the information about the layered structure is contained in ∆Φ +-. For practical applications with neutron beams, we need to consider 2 important experimental limitations. The accessible entanglement lengths ξ are in the range of 35µm, and the beam divergence is typically of the order of 10 mrad. Hence, the polarization is highly oscillating. When plotted as a function of k z , one can average (or coarse-grain) over a window including several of these oscillations leading to the effective observed polarization
div.formula: P (obs) x = 1 + cos ∆Φ +- 2 , P(obs) y
div.p: We also observe that P (obs) x

to have higher sensitivity to the magnetic and non-magnetic densities when the probed layer is contained in a specially-engineered multilayer sample to have large GH-shifts. This suggests that entangled reflectometry in TR can be utilized as complementary to the usual polarized reflectometry.

div.head: III. PROBING NON-MAGNETIC AND MAGNETIC IN-PLANE STRUCTURES
div.p: We are interested in probing non-magnetic and magnetic structures that arise on the surface of a material slab or thin film by exploiting entanglement in the quantum probe. The approach we describe next differs from the phase-object approximation [30][31][32]. To keep notation compact, hereinafter we assume the origin of time, t = 0, when Ψ (r) ξ 's centers r µν (t) will leave the surface at different times. This differs from the usual phase object approximation, but since measurements are performed in the asymptotic limit t → ∞, such initial temporal delay should not affect the final outcome. Nonetheless, the initial spatial separation between incoming coherent spinors, ξ, and the outgoing additional separation due to GH-shift, are still taken into account in our formalism. These separations show up in both amplitude Λ µµ ′ νν ′ and phase η µµ ′ νν ′ (Eq. ( 12)) at the detector.
Our approach starts with the reflected state
where the unitary operator is given by
div.formula: R = dk dr ∥ 2π R(r ∥ , k z )e -ik ∥ •r ∥ k ∥ r ∥ ⊗ |-k z ⟩⟨k z | ,(58)
div.p: and R(r ∥ , k z ) is the reflection operator derived in Sec. I. Then, its time-evolved, reflected wave packet becomes Ψ The particular case of non-magnetic samples where only inhomogeneities in the nuclear density are present in the sample, i.e., R(r ∥ , k z ) = R(r ∥ , k z )1, can be easily analyzed. The reflected wave function can be re-expressed
dr ∥ 2π e -ik ∥ •r ∥ ×R(r ∥ , k z )Ψ (i) (r ∥µ , k z , 0)e -i k (r)2 2m t |µ⟩ ê ,(60)
involving only the index µ because R(r ∥ , k z ) is diagonal, r ∥µ = r ∥µ ξ 2 , and
div.formula: Ψ (i) (r ∥µ , k z , t) = dk ∥ 2π e i(k ∥ •r ∥µ -k (r)2 2m t) Ψ (i) (k ∥ , k z ). (61
div.p: To proceed, we assume that the entanglement vector is confined to the xy-plane, i.e., ξ = ξ ∥ , and a spinecho measurement is performed where the disentangler's settings are ξ (se) = -ξ ∥ and d = ê. While details of spinecho reflectometry are given in the next section, we need the disentangler operator in Eq. (68) to write the state after the disentangler Ψ (se) ξ (r ∥ , k z , t) = ⟨r ∥ , k z |U se ê (ξ (se) )|Ψ (r)
One can then compute, e.g., the polarization
div.formula: P γ (ξ ∥ , t) = dr ∥ dk z Ψ (se) † ξ (r ∥ , k z , t)σ γ Ψ (se)
div.p: ξ (r ∥ , k z , t), γ = x, y, z, which after averaging over the impact parameters (x c , y c ) yields Eq. ( 14) for γ = x. Next, we consider the situation where in-plane inhomogeneities are magnetic. In this case, since n(r ∥ ) varies over the surface, we assume that the sample's reflection operator does not change appreciably over a distance ∆
a condition that allows us to further approximate Ψ

Note that this replacement does not preserve unitarity. Therefore, one needs to normalize Ψ (r) ξ (r, t) in Eq. (59). In addition, if the incoming wave packet is elliptical we use the quasi-spherical approximation of Eq. ( 5) (leading to a separable reflected wave packet).

The polarization of the reflected wave packet with initial impact parameter r c , P γ (r c , ξ), is calculated by substituting Eq. ( 59) into (45). The result has the form of Eq. ( 11) but with quantities, U µν , Φ ν , δpν , and W 2 ν , spatially dependent on r (r) γµ (t = 0). The observed beam polarization is averaged over the area defined by the impact parameter, r c , assumed bigger than that of the characteristic unit cell, p, of the magnetic (or birenfringet) surface structure to be probed
and it sums to 1 when ξ = 0.

As an illustration consider the case of incoming entangled wave packets spatially separated by ξ = ξ x with spin polarization |±⟩ ẑ . The centers of those wave packets experience different magnetic fields B(r which can be greater or less than 1 depending on k 0x ξ.

div.head: IV. SPIN-ECHO REFLECTOMETRY
div.p: In this section we discuss the effect of a spin-echo measurement on the observed beam polarization. See Fig. (1) for a setup.

The entangled wave packet of Eq. ( 8) is generated by an entangler, an instrument such as a pair of Magnetic Wollaston prisms or RF-flippers [33]. Operationally, the entangler can be represented as a unitary operator in position basis, with r ± = r ± ξ 2 , and |±⟩ ê eigenstates of σ • ê with ê an arbitrary quantization axis; Eq. ( 8) corresponds to ê = ẑ. In transmission mode the disentangler, which reverses the phase difference and separation of spinor components introduced by the entangler, corresponds to U se ê (ξ) † = U se ê (-ξ). To understand the action of U se ê (ξ) on an input state, consider a polarized plane wave |k⟩ ⊗ |+⟩ x. Then, the action of U se ẑ (ξ) on that state generates the spinor χ (i) k•ξ of Eq. ( 8). Similarly, if the input state is |r⟩ ⊗ |+⟩ x, U se ẑ (ξ) shifts |±⟩ ẑ 's initial positions |r⟩ to |r ± ⟩. Hence, because a polarized wave packet can be written as a superposition of either k or r, it will experience both shifts. Particularly, the imparted phase becomes the phase ∓k • ξ/2, while the position shift becomes its center shift r c ± ξ/2. Due to the displacement of the wave packet center, if one simply performs a measurement without using the disentangler, U se ẑ (-ξ), the observed polarization will generally display an exponential damping term, as |ξ| increases, due to the lack of overlap between the two spinor components. If the interaction between the beam and the sample imparts a (momentum-dependent or independent) relative phase shift between those components without changing the quantization axes (i.e., n = ê), one can always set up the disentangler with d = ê in order to cancel the ξ-dependence in the measured polarization, thus uncovering the relative phase shift. The GH-shift is associated to a momentum-dependent situation.

In the general case where the interaction with the sample also changes the incoming spinor basis |±⟩ ê, the disentangler cancellation logic above breaks down. In principle, one might attempt to change the disentangler basis (after reflection) to be the same as the sample's spinor basis, in our notation d = n, but this is impractical for the case the basis change is inhomogeneous (n(r ∥ )). In our entangled-beam reflectometry setup, the action of U se d (ξ (se) ) on our reflected wave packet |Ψ (r)

ξ ⟩, at t = t se with ξ (se) = -(ξ x , ξ y , -ξ z ), is ⟨k|U se d (ξ (se) )|Ψ --. Consequently, their wave packets' centers are also ξ-independent but differ only by their corresponding GH-shifts. This is not the case for Ψ

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. Setup for measuring the entangled GH-shift. The incoming beam traverses an entangler (E) before impinging on the surface. The reflected entangled state may be subject to a disentagler (D) before one extracts the shift from the measured polarization Pγ. The entanglement vector ξ is arbitrary and may even be purely longitudinal. The sample quantization axis is n (B1 = B1 n) with eigenspinors |±⟩ n.

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Average beam polarization P x as a function of the entanglement length ξy for two different magnetic structures with periods pa and p b (P y , P z are ξy-independent constants). One can vary the angle β, changing ξ's direction, to obtain structural correlation along different axes.

figure.figDesc: se ê (ξ) = dk |k⟩⟨k| ⊗ (e -i k•ξ 2 |+⟩ ê⟨+| + e i k•ξ 2 |-⟩ ê⟨-|),(68)in momentum basis, or equivalently asU se ê (ξ) = dr(|r + ⟩⟨r| ⊗ |+⟩ ê⟨+| + |r -⟩⟨r| ⊗ |-⟩ ê⟨-|),(69)

figure.head: ξ
figure.figDesc: (t se )⟩ = ⟨k|Ψ

Ψ (r) µν (k, t se )(e -i k (r) •ξ (se) 2 d⟨+|ν⟩ n |+⟩ d +e i k (r) •ξ (se) 2 d⟨-|ν⟩ n |-⟩ d),µν (r, t se ) with Ψ (r) µν (r, t) in Eq.(10). Let us consider first the case d ̸ = n. Then, the inner-products d⟨±|ν⟩ n contain extra phases that will show up in the measured polarization. If, on the other hand ê ̸ = d = n, then the disentangler imparts the phase ±k (r) • ξ (se) /2 = ±k • ξ to the state Ψ (r) µ± . However, from Eq. (8) the phase imparted on Ψ (r) µ± by the original entangler is -µk • ξ/2. Hence, the phases ±k • ξ/2 cancel out in Ψ

where their centers get displaced further apart because of the extra ξ-dependence. While the analysis here focuses on the Ψ (se) µν 's phases and centers, in order to assess the full impact of the entangler and disentangler on observables, such as reflectivity and polarization, these are not the only two relevant factors. Eq.(70) indicates that U µν affects the magnitude |Ψ (se) µν |, thus certain components Ψ (se) µν might contribute more significantly to observables depending on the orientation of ê and n.

div.p: Acknowledgments. We are indebted to conversations with David Baxter, Emilio Cobanera, Rebecca Dally, Jonathan Gaudet, Sam McKay, Charles Majkrzak, and Dmitry Pushin. The IU Quantum Science and Engineering Center is supported by the Office of the IU Bloomington Vice Provost for Research through its Emerging Areas of Research program. This research was undertaken thanks in part to funding from the Canada First Research Excellence Fund. GO gratefully acknowledges support from the Institute for Advanced Study.

div.head: SUPPLEMENTAL MATERIAL: ENTANGLED-BEAM REFLECTOMETRY AND GOOS-H ÄNCHEN SHIFT
Q. Le Thien, R. Pynn and G. Ortiz *
div.p: In what follows, we expand on various aspects.

div.head: I. NEUTRON OPTICS WITH SPIN
div.p: In the following we are assuming the optical potential approximation, where the medium represents a constant (spin or polarization dependent) refractive index [27,28]. The magnetic structures along the z-axis are simply characterized by a stratified medium with layers, labeled by the index j = 0, 1, • • • , N + 1, and potential energy given by Vj = V jµ n σ • B j .
Here, V j is a scalar potential, µ n < 0 for neutrons, σ = (σ x , σ y , σ z ) are Pauli matrices and bj = B j /|B j | is the magnetic field unit vector (see Fig. 3).
